erroneous_event: <error>Mother returned, surveying the wreckage, checking for collateral damage. “Ava’s stuffed kitty only clipped your shoulder, Avery, and then you toed the baby doll’s head across the floor.” she said.</error>
story:
Mother had said “not another peep,” and she meant it. Avery scowled across the playroom at Ava, who still gripped his Nintendo Switch in her cootie-filled hands. If she could touch his things without asking, he could do the same. He marched to the toy box and fished out her lilac-fairy Barbie—perfect for his reenactment of a Berlin air assault inspired by last night’s History Channel with Dad. He pinned Barbie among block-built ruins and flew a B-17 with his arms, leveling the city. The kaboom came as Barbie toppled face-first, trapped beneath the roof of the Third Reich’s building. Avery raised his hands in triumph.

Ava’s eyes narrowed to slits. She wanted to yell for Mother, but remembered the warning. Instead, she placed the Switch on the craft table near a handful of drying, topless markers and staged a counterstrike. She swiped Barbie away from Berlin, then lined up a squad of scantily clad dolls across Avery’s aircraft carrier for a sunbathing session. G.I. Joe took Cali Barbie’s hand and leaned in for a kiss. Avery answered by toppling her Barbie Dream House. The two stood, huffing and puffing, then retreated to corners under a temporary cease-fire.

They found quieter weapons. Ava scribbled “Avery is a big fathead” in teal crayon and planted it on his chair. He pretended she was the evil emperor he hunted on the Switch. They exchanged silent salvos and giggled—insults landing, no peeps made. Mother poked her head in, saw the expected battlefield of toys, smiled, and returned to the laundry.

Heels click-clacked away. Ava puffed up her stuffed kitty and pitched it at Avery. It only clipped his shoulder. Avery set the Switch carefully on a padded stack of paper. His eyes squinted, his lips scrunched. World War III loomed. He dug into the toy box for her blinking-eyed baby doll—the same one he had run through a Navy SEALs scuba test that morning. The dripping joints had started the day’s second round of trouble.

It had begun at breakfast when Ava finished the last of the Cap’n Crunch and left him plain Cheerios. While Mother sipped coffee and watched the neighbor’s backyard, Avery launched a Cheerio or two across the table, pinging Ava’s chubby cheeks. She retaliated with sugary cannonballs. Milk sloshed over the table, the floor, her pink unicorn shirt, and his camo. The ragged basset hound vacuumed up the fallout. Screams rose—8-year-old and 5-year-old barbs fired fast—until Mother stormed in, eyes sharp, and threatened to confiscate bikes, electronics, and joy, and make them sit in corners reading—yuck—books. Ava drew a breath to protest. Mother snapped her fingers shut like a beak: “Not a peep.” Rags and sponges were deployed.

Back in the playroom, Avery tipped the baby doll so its eyelids clacked open and shut, a little sinister. Then he popped the head off. It rolled to his feet. He toed it across the floor like a grenade. Ava’s fury flared. She grabbed the Switch and slammed it face down on the bare tabletop—a thrown gauntlet. A crack spidered through the small screen. A final game-over bleep sounded.

Mother returned, surveying the wreckage, checking for collateral damage. “Ava’s stuffed kitty only clipped your shoulder, Avery, and then you toed the baby doll’s head across the floor.” she said.
